# Reflection — Tag that Line

> **Note:** This feedback is AI-generated and experimental. It's meant to surface useful observations, not to grade you. Take what resonates, ignore what doesn't. Your own sense of what you learned matters more than anything here.

## Scope & Idea Clarity

**What went well:** The idea is razor-sharp — "read a tagline, pick the right poster from four options" is immediately understandable. The target user is specific and grounded (movie aficionados and theater customers, not "everyone"), and the market research identified a real gap: nobody is doing the visual tagline-to-poster match. Three specific competitors were cited (Tagline Toss Up, Movie Geek, Sporcle) to prove the gap exists.

**What to try next time:** Some scope cuts were overly cautious — the poster collage background was listed as cut but turned out to be trivially buildable (Estevan built it during the PRD conversation using three Python scripts). Next time, pressure-test cuts against what you actually know you can build, not against a time estimate.

## Requirements Thinking

**What went well:** Real expansion happened from scope to PRD. The tap-until-correct mechanic (5/3/2/0 scoring), streak multiplier rules, 3-2-1 clapboard countdown, and poster fallback were all surfaced during the PRD phase — none existed in the scope doc. Acceptance criteria are specific and testable: "Tapping the correct poster on the first try awards 5 points" is verifiable by looking at the screen.

**What to try next time:** The scoring formula and rating tier thresholds were deferred to build. Estevan pushed to dig into the math during PRD — that instinct was right. When game feel depends on specific numbers, those numbers are requirements, not implementation details. An extra deepening round here could have locked down the formula earlier.

## Technical Decisions

**What went well:** The Firestore-to-memory caching pattern — Estevan's idea — was the key architectural decision. One API call per session, zero Firestore reads during gameplay. The architecture traces directly to PRD requirements, and the decision to keep scoring client-side was instant and correct given no auth and no leaderboard.

**What to try next time:** The spec called for `@lobby-suite/content-db` for Firestore access, but the build hit cross-project SDK issues in Cloud Functions, requiring a pivot to the Firestore REST API. The pivot was handled cleanly, but it could have been caught during spec if a deepening round had probed deployment environment constraints — specifically, how reading from another project's Firestore behaves in Cloud Functions.

## Plan vs. Reality

**What went well:** 10 of 10 checklist items completed. The file structure matches the spec exactly — every component, hook, service, and route landed where planned. The scoring logic implements the PRD's 5/3/2/0 scheme with streak multiplier precisely as specified. Where drift happened (REST API replacing content-db, step 1 becoming verification instead of seeding), it was adapted to cleanly and documented in process notes.

**What to try next time:** The demo mode easter egg was a smart addition for the Devpost submission but wasn't in the plan. Capturing emergent ideas in iteration notes keeps the checklist accurate as a record of planned vs. discovered work.

## How You Worked

**What went well:** Estevan drove every major decision across all phases. Format choice, cut list, scoring mechanic, caching architecture, decoy pool expansion, client-side scoring, data-first build sequencing — all originated from the learner. He pushed back when the process was wrong (scoring belongs in requirements, not deferred to build), built the poster collage independently during a planning conversation, handled all Firebase/GCP admin tasks without assistance, and caught the poster cropping issue during checkpoint review.

**What to try next time:** Deepening rounds were used sparingly (1 on scope, 0 on PRD/spec/checklist). Estevan's instincts are strong enough that mandatory questions covered a lot of ground, but the Firestore cross-project issue is exactly the kind of thing a spec deepening round catches. Investing one extra round on /spec next time would be high-value.

## Your Goals

Estevan came in wanting to formalize the "vibe coding" proof-of-concept workflow into something rigorous and repeatable, and to build habits that make corporate IT deployments defensible. The rigor showed up everywhere: specific acceptance criteria, documented architecture rationale, traceable decisions from scope through deployed app. The security angle didn't get heavy exercise in this particular build (no auth, no user data), but the habit of documenting and defending decisions — the muscle that makes IT conversations go smoothly — got a real workout. The process landed: "Nothing surprised me — we got exactly what we drew out."

## Reflection

Estevan's key takeaway: the hackathon time constraint was artificially limiting. He proved it by building the poster collage background during the PRD conversation using existing infrastructure and three Python scripts. For builders with established ecosystems and agent workflows, scoping to ambition rather than a clock produces better results.

Two pieces of process feedback for the plugin itself: (1) Scoring mechanics are requirements, not implementation details — the process should support deeper exploration of game-feel math during PRD, not defer it. (2) The git repository should be initialized at /onboard, not at the end. Builders who are pulling from existing infrastructure across projects need a way to share config and assets from the start. On a freshly restored workstation, missing system dependencies (gcloud CLI) also caused friction during deploy — a dependency check during onboard would save time.
